My first time on Axis foils! Art V2 999
AXIS Foils: What's Hot in 5 at the AWSI

posts.source_label: Axis foil range review 2025
posts.text: The PNG 1150 was the original pump foil. AXIS hit this early when other brands didn't even have pump foiling on their radar. It was the benchmark for dock starting and flatwater pumping for a very long time. Good for lightweight winging and riding tiny bumps.
posts.key_insight: Was the industry benchmark for dock starting and flatwater pumping before other brands had pump foils on their radar

posts.source_label: Axis foil range review 2025
posts.text: The original PNG 1300 was much more high aspect than the rest of the PNG range. More glidy, faster to pump, but without as aggressive lift. Took time to learn for dock starting but became the reviewer's preferred pumping foil due to its extra glide and speed over the 1150.
posts.key_insight: Higher aspect design within PNG range made it faster and glidier than the 1150, ultimately preferred for pumping once the dock start technique was cracked

posts.source_label: Axis foil range review 2025
posts.text: The PNG 1310 has the most early lift at low speeds of any foil in the entire AXIS range ever produced. Incredible glide on anything. Great for heavyweight riders wanting to practice sub paddle-ups. However, it has an aggressive stall point and can throw you off if you carve too quickly or are slightly off balance at dock start.
posts.key_insight: Has the most early lift at low speeds of any foil AXIS has ever produced — ideal for heavyweights doing sub paddle-ups

posts.source_label: Axis foil range review 2025
posts.text: The PNG V2 1200 is a really good upgrade on the 1150 — does everything it did but with better maneuverability and is actually more forgiving in every aspect including dock starting and pumping. Easy to recover from mistakes, very controllable. A little slow but that's by design. Great for beginners, relaxing winging, or small wave SUP foiling.
posts.key_insight: More forgiving than the original 1150 in every aspect while adding better maneuverability — the ideal relaxed all-rounder

posts.source_label: Axis foil range review 2025
posts.text: PNG V2 1300 has aspect ratio 10.36 vs 8 on the 1200. It pumps faster, glides very nicely, and is even easier to dock start than the V2 1200. More locked-in feel with less maneuverability, but still turns if you're decisive. Amazing for very small surf, micro bumps, bay run downwinding, and super light wind winging at 80kg.
posts.key_insight: Higher aspect ratio (10.36) pumps faster than the 1200 and is possibly even easier to dock start — a versatile do-it-all for 80kg riders

posts.source_label: Axis foil range review 2025
posts.text: The PNG V2 1400 is the easiest foil the reviewer has ever dock started. Crazy consistency — a solid platform to land on and pump away. Staying high on the mast is easy and pumping feels like less effort than normal. The caveat: at 80kg it's beginning to get unforgiving if balance is off or you don't control exits from turns carefully. Heavier riders (100kg+) will find the extra weight helps control it.
posts.key_insight: Easiest foil ever dock started by this experienced reviewer, but at 80kg it's at the limit of controllability — heavier riders benefit from the extra weight for control

posts.source_label: Axis foil range review 2025
posts.text: The Art Pro 1201 (AR 11.14) was a long-time favorite for pumping because of how much you could accelerate with it. A really good balance between top end and bottom end — surprisingly good low end for a foil with really good glide. Now superseded by the PNG V2 1300 for the reviewer.
posts.key_insight: Was the go-to pump foil for acceleration, but has been superseded by the PNG V2 1300 which offers better all-round performance

posts.source_label: Axis foil range review 2025
posts.text: The Art Pro 1401 took over from the PNG 1310 for longest pump duration. Could link more tiny microbumps than the 1310 on really small days. Less unforgiving of imbalances than the 1310, though still fairly stiff in maneuverability. Now superseded by the PNG V2 1400 and 1300.
posts.key_insight: Beat the PNG 1310 for linking tiny microbumps in small conditions, but has since been superseded by PNG V2 1300/1400

posts.source_label: Axis foil range review 2025
posts.text: The Art Pro 1001 was the reviewer's favorite all-round foil in the Art Pro range. Incredible for winging — speed, maneuverability, and glide were all outstanding. Was always the first choice if conditions allowed.
posts.key_insight: Best all-round foil in the Art Pro range — exceptional blend of speed, maneuverability, and glide for winging

posts.source_label: Axis foil range review 2025
posts.text: The Fireball 1350 is the biggest in the current range (AR 13.93). Can be dock started from static position. Amazing glide, pretty fast. Turns well if you commit decisively with a stiff mast (Ultra Pro carbon). May feel wobbly for lighter riders or with aluminium mast due to wingspan. For light wind winging, sub paddle-ups, and any downwind discipline in small conditions.
posts.key_insight: Can be dock started from static at 80kg — amazing glide at AR 13.93, but requires Ultra Pro mast for stability at that wingspan

posts.source_label: Axis foil range review 2025
posts.text: The Fireball range is a game changer. The under-camber design gives progressive, gentle lift without overfoiling or nasty surprises, plus a gentle stall point with plenty of warning. They operate across a much wider range of conditions than any previous AXIS foil — you can handle both small pumping sections and ramped-up big conditions on the same downwinder. The 1160, 1070, and 1000 are the reviewer's main rotation for prone, surfing, and downwinding.
posts.key_insight: Under-camber design delivers the widest usable condition range of any AXIS foil ever — progressive lift, gentle stall, no nasty surprises

posts.source_label: Axis foil range review 2025
posts.text: The Fireball 940 and 880 are amazing for winging at the reviewer's skill level. The 940 is the power winging choice — incredible performance. At these smaller sizes the Fireballs are still remarkably pumpable for their size.
posts.key_insight: The 940 is the go-to power winging foil — incredible performance in smaller Fireball sizes that still pump surprisingly well

posts.source_label: Axis foil range review 2025
posts.text: Art V2s are faster than the originals, turn better, and tolerate wing tip breaches better. They don't feel 'surfy' — more like a cross between high aspect and surf foil. They cut through messy conditions without wobble. The 1099 is the biggest, still an amazing performance foil for winging, surfing, and downwinding. If forced to pick one Swiss Army knife foil from the entire AXIS range, it would be the Art V2s.
posts.key_insight: The Art V2 range is the Swiss Army knife of the AXIS lineup — handles messy conditions best, cuts through everything without wobble

posts.source_label: Axis foil range review 2025
posts.text: The Art V2 979 and 879 are the reviewer's first choice for the messy, chaotic North Sea conditions with multiple sandbars and currents. They handle speed, cut through everything without wobble, and excel where it's inconsistent, messy, but powerful. The go-to foils when conditions are too chaotic for Spitfires.
posts.key_insight: First choice for chaotic, messy conditions — handle extreme speed and cut through rough water without wobble where Spitfires can't cope

posts.source_label: Axis foil range review 2025
posts.text: Spitfires hold a special place — they marked a turning point for AXIS. Even the big sizes feel nice to turn with that lovely surfy feel. The 1180 has a huge usable range. The 960 and 1030 took over from PNG 1010/910 for surf use. The 900 was a standout — perfect for good surf days and mediocre conditions alike. Best demo foil for events — incredibly user-friendly yet capable. Perfect for weak, mushy waves.
posts.key_insight: Even the large sizes retain a lovely surfy feel — the 1180 has such a huge usable range that the 1100 in between became unnecessary

posts.source_label: Axis foil range review 2025
posts.text: The Tempo 1020 with titanium fuselage is futuristic — build quality and performance off the charts. Speed and glide are limitless, but it feels confident and controlled, not dangerous. Incredibly loose on the roll axis — keeps rolling until you stop it — which requires good foil control skills. Not a beginner foil, but far more accessible than just the elite. For downwinding and power winging it's amazing. Pumps more efficiently than Fireballs at speed but Fireballs are easier for flat water pumping.
posts.key_insight: Limitless speed feels controlled not dangerous — the roll axis is incredibly loose (keeps rolling until you stop it) but is learnable in 10-20 minutes

posts.source_label: AXIS Tempo & Surge Deep Dive with Adrian Roper
posts.text: The Tempo required a completely new titanium fuselage because the wings are so thin (<10mm) they couldn't fit on the existing black fuselage. The expansion plate system achieves the stiffest joint ever for holding wings. All Tempo wings are 16 aspect ratio. The wings use uni-directional carbon on the outside (replacing traditional woven carbon), making them 25% stiffer. Removing woven carbon from the mast gained 1.1mm thickness reduction and noticeably improved performance.
posts.key_insight: Replacing woven carbon with uni-directional carbon on wing exterior delivered 25% more stiffness — a construction breakthrough that enables even higher aspect designs

posts.source_label: AXIS Tempo & Surge Deep Dive with Adrian Roper
posts.text: Adrian raced the 920 Tempo for all Hawaii races and never felt he needed anything bigger. He ended up wishing he'd used the smaller 890 for all races. The speed and carry is 'actually insane.' For France, used the 1020 (650 area) as the go-to when conditions are questionable — its range covers 145s up to 230s comfortably. Nine out of top 20 riders in the Maui race series were on AXIS Tempos.
posts.key_insight: Designer raced the 920 in Hawaii but wished he'd used the even smaller 890 — the 1020 at just 650 area covers a range from 145s to 230s

posts.source_label: AXIS Tempo & Surge Deep Dive with Adrian Roper
posts.text: Comparing the Tempo 1020 to the Fireball 1000: the Tempo gets up about the same speed but the stall speed is actually a lot better — it goes much slower AND much faster. It's a lot less wing area (650 vs 773) but gives a whole lot more range. The reduced drag means it just carries on running when you back off power. Riders coming from Fireball to Tempo found it no harder to use but much faster. Adrian put Thiago Campos on a Fireball 1000 he'd never used before a race — he said 'Perfect.' Next year, upgraded him to the Tempo 1020 on race day, also unused — totally comfortable.
posts.key_insight: Tempo 1020 (650 area) matches Fireball 1000 (773 area) for getting up but has better stall speed AND higher top speed — area comparison is meaningless, use span

posts.source_label: AXIS Tempo & Surge Deep Dive with Adrian Roper
posts.text: The Tempo 1090 works surprisingly well for winging — going upwind, popping on a swell and surfing off downwind. Being 16 aspect ratio means very low drag. Roxy (Shannon's daughter) learned to downwind on the 1090 and found it super easy. Multiple demo riders asked 'why wouldn't I just use this as my general winging foil?' Despite looking ridiculously small in area, it's not hard to ride.
posts.key_insight: A learning downwinder found the 1090 super easy despite 16 aspect ratio — demo riders consistently questioned why they wouldn't just use it for all winging

posts.source_label: AXIS Tempo & Surge Deep Dive with Adrian Roper
posts.text: Luke (100kg team rider) has ridden the Surge 890 for 6+ months across various comps and conditions. It's his do-it-all for sub paddle, para-winging, winging, and prone foiling. The lift is super comfortable and gradual — no aggressive lift surprise. Coming off the Spitfire 780, he can surf the Surge 890 even harder AND pump it 10 times longer. The upturned wing tips handle tip breaches beautifully — bubbles wash off before reaching the fuselage. Compared to the Spitfire, it handles turbulence and white water far better — 'Spitfire feels like driving down a gravel road with no suspension.'
posts.key_insight: At 100kg, the Surge 890 surfs harder than the Spitfire 780 AND pumps 10x longer — handles turbulence and white water far better than Spitfire

posts.source_label: AXIS Tempo & Surge Deep Dive with Adrian Roper
posts.text: The Surge uses the same high-camber foil section as the Fireball (3.7% camber) for great lift and glide, with a straight median line for consistent handling. The 'gross mustache' turn-down/turn-up wing tips make it feel narrower, roll easier, breach better, and even aid re-entry after jumps. A flat center section keeps it progressive in turns without locking in. Tested back-to-back with a standard Fireball 1000 — the winglet version was slightly slower to get up and had lower top speed, but felt narrower and rolled easier.
posts.key_insight: The 'gross mustache' wing tips create a narrower feel and easier roll while dramatically improving tip breach recovery and re-entry from jumps

posts.source_label: AXIS Tempo & Surge Deep Dive with Adrian Roper
posts.text: Sven (heavy rider, ~100kg) uses the Surge 950 for surf-downwind and the 1010 for smaller conditions. In knee-high waves at Poort Beach with the 1010, it was incredibly fun. Compared to the Spitfire 1030, the Surge 1010 has more lift (gets up earlier), and the stall speed is 'insane' — you can almost be standing still and recover with front foot pressure. Full tip breach recovery was unprecedented — breached the entire foil out of the water with a para-wing and it rolled back perfectly.
posts.key_insight: Full foil breach recovery that was 'unbelievable' — breached entire foil out of water and it rolled back in perfectly, something never experienced before

posts.source_label: AXIS Tempo & Surge Deep Dive with Adrian Roper
posts.text: When asked what front wing to choose between ART V2s, Surge, and Spitfires for non-downwind surfing: 'Surge every time, every day of the week.' For matching sizes, size down from ART V2: if on ART V2 999, look at Surge 890 or 950. If on ART V2 819, the Surge 830 is a good transition. Speed is very impressive when correctly sized. Once he jumped on the Surge for proning, he never went back to the Spitfire.
posts.key_insight: Size transition guide: ART V2 999 → Surge 890/950, ART V2 819 → Surge 830 — once on Surge for prone, never went back to Spitfire

posts.source_label: AXIS Tempo & Surge Deep Dive with Adrian Roper
posts.text: Luke's main surf tail is the Surf Skinny 300 — pumps better than Progressives, turns better than Skinnies, and turns as well as a Progressive. Found the regular Skinnies drew out turns too much. The 300 is the perfect gap-filler. Kai and Perth prefer the 280, Sven (heavier) rides the 320.
posts.key_insight: Surf Skinny 300 tail is the sweet spot — pumps better than Progressives, turns better than Skinnies — size by weight: 280 for lighter, 300 mid, 320 for heavy

posts.source_label: AXIS Tempo & Surge Deep Dive with Adrian Roper
posts.text: Adrian strongly argues against comparing foils by area. Three foils with 1000mm span — ART V2 999 (1024 area), Fireball 1000 (773 area), Tempo 1020 (650 area) — all get up exactly the same. The Tempo 650 area has better bottom end AND top end than the 1024 area ART V2. The only real comparison method is running CFD on exact design files. Span is a better guideline than area.
posts.key_insight: Three 1000mm-span foils with areas of 1024, 773, and 650 all get up the same — area comparison is meaningless with modern high-camber designs

posts.source_label: Axis Surge, ART V2, Spitfire and Fireball compared while wingfoiling
posts.text: The Surge 890 became an immediate favorite after the first session. It sits perfectly between the Spitfire and ART V2 with unique characteristics of its own. Extremely forgiving and stable — you can continuously adjust line and angle without stalling even in messy water. Shines when pushed hard in turns using wave energy. Excellent pop for jumping. Not as fast as ART V2 but similar glide, and can ride at lower speeds in the same size.
posts.key_insight: You can continuously adjust line and foil angle without stalling even in messy water — the forgiveness really shines when pushing hard turns

posts.source_label: Axis Surge, ART V2, Spitfire and Fireball compared while wingfoiling
posts.text: The ART V2 879 is the standout size in the range. It keeps up with fast moving waves while still allowing fairly tight turns. Excels at riding swell and feels good staying in the pocket of fast breaking waves. Generates a lot of speed, making it great for jumping. On smaller days, switches to the 939 or 999 for better glide at lower speeds. The ART V2 999 feels a bit slower than the V1, and with V2s you generally want to size down by one to match speed.
posts.key_insight: The 879 is the standout size — keeps up with fast waves while still allowing tight turns; V2s generally need to size down by one vs V1 for comparable speed

posts.source_label: Axis Surge, ART V2, Spitfire and Fireball compared while wingfoiling
posts.text: The Spitfire 780 is perfect for overhead powerful waves where you don't need as much glide. Great turning ability on a wave. Larger Spitfires (900+) impress with easy get-up making them excellent for newer riders. However, due to less glide than the Surge, the reviewer would now choose the Surge over the Spitfire in most conditions. When waves are fast and you can't keep up on a Spitfire, switch to an ART V2.
posts.key_insight: Still the best choice for overhead powerful waves, but the Surge has replaced it for most conditions due to superior glide

posts.source_label: Axis Surge, ART V2, Spitfire and Fireball compared while wingfoiling
posts.text: The smaller Fireballs (880 etc.) aren't a favorite for local conditions with lots of water movement — very high aspect foils don't lock in well when going upwind while winging and stall more quickly. However, the Fireball 1250 is an amazing favorite for light wind downwind para-winging. It excels in glide, gets up on foil at very low speeds, and is ideal for calm water sessions where you can bump ride endlessly. Prefers smooth drawn-out turns rather than snappy ones.
posts.key_insight: The 1250 is amazing for light wind downwind para-winging — excels in glide and low-speed get-up; smaller Fireballs don't lock in well upwind in choppy conditions

posts.source_label: AWSI - Axis 2026: New Products
posts.text: The Tempo 890 (495 area, 16 aspect ratio) is the smallest Tempo and what the twins and Kai Thompson raced in Maui. Nine out of the top 20 riders were on Axis Tempos. The layup has been improved since Maui with much stiffer wings — you can barely bend the tip. A 16 aspect wing requires a thin narrow fuselage system; a bulky carbon fuselage simply won't work. The titanium system's expansion plate solved the earlier tab fitting issues from the Hawaii races.
posts.key_insight: The race-winning 890 at just 495 area and 16 AR dominated Maui — post-race layup improvements made wings even stiffer with barely bendable tips

posts.source_label: AWSI - Axis 2026: New Products
posts.text: The 1750 span dock start pump wing has over 20 aspect ratio with about 1500 sq area. Requires the new fatty mast (18.1mm thick, 11.7mm bend vs 38mm for aluminum) and fatty fuselage for control. Without the fatty mast, you're just trying to balance rather than steer. They've built even bigger wings that still have good control with this system. Years of experimentation before release — Adrian refused to release until totally happy.
posts.key_insight: At 20+ aspect ratio and 1750 span, the fatty mast (11.7mm bend vs 38mm aluminum) transforms it from unrideable to fully controllable and steerable

posts.source_label: AWSI - Axis 2026: New Products
posts.text: The 1500 span dock start wing (17 aspect ratio) is the foil Niko Itan used to set a world record: 1 hour of dock start pumping covering 20.12km — averaging 20.12 km/h for the entire hour. Made of ultra high modulus carbon because it's so thin. Many dock start wings can't even reach that speed, let alone sustain it for an hour.
posts.key_insight: World record foil: Niko Itan dock-start pumped 20.12km in 1 hour on this wing — most dock start foils can't even reach that average speed

posts.source_label: AWSI - Axis 2026: New Products
posts.text: A new downwind-specific mast called the 'Kiwi' is in development — only 11mm thick at the bottom. Created by sanding woven carbon off Ultra Pro masts during Maui race prep, which removed 1.1mm thickness and dramatically improved up-and-overs. The gold logo version commemorates the Maui race series. Designed specifically for under-90kg riders using the Tempo range — not for dock start with big wings.
posts.key_insight: The Kiwi mast at 11mm thick is minimum-drag downwind-specific — born from race prep experimentation of sanding woven carbon off existing masts

posts.source_label: AXIS PNG 1400 V2 Review and Comparison vs PNG 1300 V2
posts.text: First impression of the PNG V2 1400 is extreme stability, especially coming directly from the 1300. Immediately slower than the 1300 but noticeably more efficient — less energy per pump to go forward. AXIS works with camber rather than just going thicker and slower, keeping the pumping movement natural. Speed range is still quite good despite the size. The board points slightly down when gliding, suggesting effective profile design with a very flat angle of attack.
posts.key_insight: More efficient than the 1300 — uses less energy per pump while maintaining a surprisingly good speed range through camber design rather than just adding thickness

posts.source_label: AXIS PNG 1400 V2 Review and Comparison vs PNG 1300 V2
posts.text: The PNG V2 1400 is best for beginners/intermediates over 90kg who want long distance pumping. Lighter riders should start on the 1300 instead because big foils are hard to recover from rolls. You lose carving ability vs the 1300 but gain significant long-distance efficiency. The reviewer considers this 'really the limit' for useful foil size — beyond this you'd need a dedicated beta foil. Recommends longer fuselage and standard stab for beginners, not the ultra-short setups.
posts.key_insight: Sweet spot between versatility and long distance — the practical size limit for useful pump foils; over 90kg → 1400, lighter → stick with 1300

posts.source_label: AXIS PNG 1400 V2 Review and Comparison vs PNG 1300 V2
posts.text: The PNG V2 1300 turns slightly better than the 1400 (roughly 6.5/10 vs ~5/10 for turning) but the difference isn't huge since neither carves sharply. The 1300 is better for waves and wake riding. The 1400 is noticeably better for long-distance pumping efficiency. For lighter riders, the 1300 is the recommended starting point as the 1400's big span makes roll recovery very difficult at light weights.
posts.key_insight: The carving difference between 1300 and 1400 is only 1-2 points out of 10 — the 1400's long-distance advantage is more significant than the turning penalty

posts.source_label: Honest Review: Axis Fireballs 1500 & 1750
posts.text: The Fireball 1750 is exceptional at 175cm span with aspect ratio 20.12. Incredibly pitch sensitive and NOT a beginner foil. Requires lots of training to find the sweet spot — not fitness training but technique training. With the right touch, pumping feels effortless with very little movement. Must use fatty mast for stiffness due to the wingspan — you want a stiff connection, not flex. Can still turn despite its size, probably due to small wing tips. World record holder Niko Itan uses these with a customized smaller stab.
posts.key_insight: AR 20.12 is incredibly pitch sensitive requiring technique mastery, not fitness — effortless pumping once dialed but needs fatty mast for stiffness

posts.source_label: Honest Review: Axis Fireballs 1500 & 1750
posts.text: The Fireball 1500 with ultrashort (fatty) fuse and skinny 30 stab is less pitch sensitive than the 1750 setup while still pumpable. Reviewer's personal pick over the 1750: go with psycho short fuse + smaller tail for more efficiency and more practice-reward. Both are around $3000 total (roughly one franc per cm of wingspan). Build quality is excellent — stiff foils with great protective covers featuring trailing edge lashes and tip pockets.
posts.key_insight: Reviewer picks the 1500 over 1750 with psycho short fuse + smaller tail — less pitch sensitivity while maintaining efficiency

posts.source_label: AXIS Tempo Foil | Real World Test | Downwind RAW Ep 007
posts.text: The Tempo 960 at 575 cm² and 16 aspect ratio has amazing bottom end for its size — paddle-up is surprisingly easy. The glide is "out of control" and connects dots effortlessly. Average speed sits around 30 km/h without trying. Not just for pros — weekend warriors who like tracking PBs will love it. Smashed all previous personal bests when switching from Fireballs to Tempo. Most riders describe it as a Fireball but quicker, less drag, slipperier. Flows through dead zones with zero pumping needed. Cutting upwind in crosswind conditions was remarkable. Made the beach finish on a tiny wing where it would normally fail.
posts.key_insight: Averages 30 km/h effortlessly and smashed all PBs from Fireballs — like a Fireball but quicker with less drag, accessible for weekend warriors not just pros

posts.source_label: The Deep Dive: AXIS ART V2 foil review | Foiling Magazine
posts.text: The ART V2 launched in 879/939/999 sizes. Looks like a refined Spitfire on a serious diet — thinner profile, higher aspect, sharper nose vs the Spitfire's bulbous front. Far improved glide and speed range over Spitfire, eliminating the Spitfire's speed limitation. Amazing bottom end: lifts early, progressive and predictable stall that tells you it's reaching limits rather than collapsing. Brilliant for winging. Pumps really well and connects lumps amazingly for prone. Rolls much better than previous ART with less paddle into wing tip.
posts.key_insight: Eliminates the Spitfire speed limitation while adding predictable progressive stall characteristics — the stall tells you rather than collapsing suddenly

posts.source_label: The Deep Dive: AXIS ART V2 foil review | Foiling Magazine
posts.text: Tip breach is a bone of contention on the ART V2. The flat foil with only a little downturn is designed for riding flat with loose roll. If you commit to a big tip breach it's great, but small accidental breaches can catch you out. Less forgiving than the Spitfire on tip breach. Best suited to cruising arcing turns rather than hard pushing carves — higher span means it prefers wider turns. Still feels relatively agile for a higher aspect foil.
posts.key_insight: Tip breach less forgiving than Spitfire — big commits are fine but small accidental breaches catch you out; best for arcing turns not hard carves

posts.source_label: The Deep Dive: AXIS ART V2 foil review | Foiling Magazine
posts.text: The ART V2 has probably the widest versatility range in the entire AXIS lineup. If forced to pick one foil for intermediate+ riders across all genres (winging, prone, downwind), this would be it. Bridges the gap between the Spitfire (too slow for downwind) and the ART Pro. The 999 is classic-sized and feels like it has a much wider range than the original ART. A great step up for existing AXIS system owners. Shorter fuse (crazy short ~600mm) makes it much more comfortable vs ultra short which felt pitch sensitive.
posts.key_insight: Widest versatility in the AXIS range — the one-foil quiver pick for intermediate+ riders; crazy short fuse (~600mm) recommended over ultra short for comfort

posts.source_label: The Deep Dive: AXIS Power Carbon Pro Ultra High Modulus Mast | Foiling Magazine
posts.text: The Power Carbon Pro Ultra High Modulus Mast is 25% stiffer than the existing High Modulus which was already an industry benchmark. Tapers to just 13.5mm at the tip. Widest point of the section moved back for reduced drag. Uses military-grade carbon. Performance gains are massive — pushes through harsh turns without cavitation where the old mast would cavitate. More rigid pumping feel, better with bigger foils. Made foils feel newer and boards feel better. "Didn't want it to be that much better, but it is a massive step up." Available in 80/90/105 cm.
posts.key_insight: 25% stiffer than already benchmark-setting High Modulus mast — eliminates cavitation in hard turns and transforms the feel of every foil mounted on it

posts.source_label: 2024 AWSI Boards Sports Expo - Axis Fireball Foils
posts.text: The Fireball launched at AWSI 2024 in four sizes: 880, 940, 1000, and 1070. Shares the same foil section as the PNG 1300 pump glide. Features 3.6% camber over the whole wing with good reflex out the back. Produces so much lift that AXIS recommends riders scale down at least one size from a similarly-sized ART Pro. Three larger sizes were announced as in development. Primary focus: winging, pumping, and downwinding. Super fast, relatively easy to get going, with endless glide.
posts.key_insight: Scale down at least one size from ART Pro due to high-camber lift — same foil section as PNG 1300 with 3.6% camber produces exceptional lift for the area

posts.source_label: My first time on Axis foils! Art V2 999
posts.text: First-ever session on any AXIS foil, using the ART V2 999 in very small waves (almost downwind conditions). Perfect glide that connected everything. Big enough to turn back into the wind and pump through to connect another wave. Wing tip breach was super smooth and easy. Setup: Foil Drive Assist Max (high power motor), Zapple 50 38L board, 80cm CERS aluminum mast, skinny AXIS tail. No complaints at all from this first session.
posts.key_insight: First-time AXIS rider had zero complaints — perfect glide in tiny waves, smooth tip breach, big enough to pump back upwind and connect waves

posts.source_label: AXIS Surge Foil Ramblings (Maxtrack UK)
posts.text: Surge is the #1 recommendation for UK/Northern Europe riders. 9.5 aspect ratio sits between Spitfire (7-8) and ART V2 (10). After first trying it in America, ran straight to Adrian and said stop building everything else - this is all we need for UK conditions.
posts.key_insight: UK distributor endorsement: Surge is the single most useful foil for UK beach break and wing foiling conditions

posts.source_label: AXIS Surge Foil Ramblings (Maxtrack UK)
posts.text: Best turning Axis foil ever. Previous foils (Spitfire, ART V2) turn on the Y-axis (yaw) but lack smooth roll. Surge has progressive roll - goes as far as you want with as much effort as you put in, like a surfboard. This roll characteristic is the #1 new feature.
posts.key_insight: Surge introduces surfboard-like progressive roll that previous AXIS foils lacked - biggest single upgrade

posts.source_label: AXIS Surge Foil Ramblings (Maxtrack UK)
posts.text: Surge 890 allowed Freddie (80kg, self-described poor pumper) to comfortably link 2-3 waves. Key improvement: forward drive in the pump - borrowed from downwind racing foils. You get forward momentum, not just staying high on the mast. Works across all sizes without needing to go bigger.
posts.key_insight: Surge brings downwind-racing forward drive into a surf foil - pumping accelerates forward, not just maintains height

posts.source_label: AXIS Surge Foil Ramblings (Maxtrack UK)
posts.text: Surge vs Fireball: Fireball wins for all-out speed/glide and fast-moving open water swell. Surge is not the fastest but does not need to be. For lake blasting at max speed, Fireball. For beach break waves and most UK conditions, Surge. Both now the two main pillars of the AXIS range.
posts.key_insight: Surge and Fireball are now the two main AXIS pillars - Surge for surf/waves, Fireball for speed/glide

posts.source_label: AXIS Surge Foil Ramblings (Maxtrack UK)
posts.text: Freddie 80kg: 890 for power winging and light wind winging, 830 for powered conditions or bigger surf, 740 on demo for toe foiling when 830 is overpowered. 1010 and 950 getting great reviews from subfoilers and Scottish loch riders on shorter sharper bumps.
posts.key_insight: 80kg rider sizing: 890 for light wind/power winging, 830 for powered conditions, 1010/950 praised for sub-foil and loch riding

posts.source_label: AXIS Surge Foil Ramblings (Maxtrack UK)
posts.text: Surf Skinny rear wing fills the gap between Skinnies (fast/DW) and Progressives (slow/turning). 280 pairs with big foils needing less tail stability, 300 is the goldilocks sweet spot, 320 for smaller foils at speed needing more stability. Amplifies the Surge forward drive feel in the pump.
posts.key_insight: Surf Skinny 300 is the goldilocks rear wing for Surge; 280 for big front wings, 320 for small front wings at speed

posts.source_label: AXIS Surge Foil Ramblings (Maxtrack UK)
posts.text: Ultra Short Advance Plus fuselage is the goldilocks fuselage for nearly everything: open water winging, prone, toe foiling. Go shorter for quicker turns; go to Short for big span foils or toe foiling big conditions. Otherwise Ultra Short Advance Plus ticks too many boxes.
posts.key_insight: Ultra Short Advance Plus is the recommended all-around fuselage for most AXIS setups per UK distributor

posts.source_label: Whats New in Foiling Feb 2026 (Surf FX Australia)
posts.text: Fireball 1750 is 170cm wingspan - as tall as a 6'2" person. Requires the Fatty mast/fuselage for stiffness at that span. Lighter riders will feel flex with regular mast. Sydney rider confirmed: if windy or a lighter rider, definitely get the Fatty.
posts.key_insight: Fireball 1750 REQUIRES Fatty mast/fuselage - lighter riders WILL feel flex with standard mast at 170cm wingspan

posts.source_label: Whats New in Foiling Feb 2026 (Surf FX Australia)
posts.text: Surge finally back in stock at Surf FX - first 3 shipments sold out before arrival. Handles tip breaches well with less cavitation - built for aggressive hard surfing with tips out. 80kg rider on Surge 830 in large surf with Foil Drive Fusion performed brilliantly.
posts.key_insight: Surge sold out 3 consecutive shipments before arrival at world-leading Australian shop; built for aggressive tip-breach surfing

posts.source_label: Whats New in Foiling Feb 2026 (Surf FX Australia)
posts.text: AXIS integrated mast: first shipment sold out immediately, back in stock after Chinese New Year 2026. Two constructions: High Modulus (lighter riders, smaller span wings) and Ultra High Modulus (bigger span, stiffer). Para-winging demand is driving integrated mast sales hard.
posts.key_insight: AXIS integrated mast sold out instantly on first shipment; choose HM for smaller/lighter setups, UHM for big span

posts.source_label: AXIS Foils: What's Hot in 5 at the AWSI
posts.text: Tempo titanium fuselage: development started when Fireball launched. Titanium body with stainless steel top plate, grub screws expand internally to lock wings - no external bolts on foil surface. 16 aspect ratio wings are ultra-high-modulus carbon with no core (too thin). Aluminum adapter included for existing rear wings. AWSI 2025 Hawaii: 9 of top 20 riders on Tempo.
posts.key_insight: Tempo titanium fuselage uses internal expansion locking - no surface bolts, 16AR wings too thin for any core material

posts.source_label: AXIS Foils: What's Hot in 5 at the AWSI
posts.text: New AXIS Foil Drive pocket trench board: battery slots under flush cover with only 2mm clearance each side - minimal drag. Quick swap: loosen two screws, slide back, lift out, dry, reinsert. Integrated mast in HM and UHM. Fiberglass in battery and mast areas for RF signal. Pre-preg carbon at 130C, closed-cell foam - no vent needed, safe in sun.
posts.key_insight: New Foil Drive pocket board: 2mm trench clearance, fiberglass signal windows, closed-cell construction - purpose-built for minimal drag
